source.note: Authored as the canonical CardForge sample. Demonstrates a complete, classroom-ready Grade 6 statistics package built from a job file.

card.title: Mean, Median, Mode, and Range
card.objective: I can find the mean, median, mode, and range of a data set.
card.languageObjective: I can explain which measure I used with the words mean, median, mode, range, and outlier.
card.description: Sample CardForge package: find the mean, median, mode, and range of a data set, with ESOL vocabulary supports, two modeled examples, practice, and an exit ticket.

lesson.overview: Students learn four ways to summarize a set of numbers — mean, median, mode, and range — and decide which one best describes a typical value, especially when an outlier is present.
lesson.objective: I can find the mean, median, mode, and range of a data set.
lesson.languageObjective: I can explain which measure I used with the words mean, median, mode, range, and outlier.
materials: Optional: sticky notes for ordering data

pacing.what: Order a short list of numbers least to greatest.
Teacher works both modeled examples aloud.

lesson.warmUp: Put these numbers in order from least to greatest: 7, 2, 9, 4, 2. (Answer: 2, 2, 4, 7, 9.) Which number appears most often?

vocabulary.definition: The average. Add all the numbers, then divide by how many there are.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El promedio. Suma todos los números y divide entre cuántos hay.

vocabulary.definition: The middle number when the data is in order.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número del medio cuando los datos están en orden.

vocabulary.definition: The number that appears most often.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número que aparece más veces.

vocabulary.definition: The biggest number minus the smallest number.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número mayor menos el número menor.

vocabulary.definition: A number much bigger or smaller than the rest.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número mucho mayor o menor que los demás.

formulas: mean = (sum of all values) ÷ (how many values)
median = middle value in order (if two middles, average them)
mode = value that appears most often (can be none or more than one)

modeledExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median, mode, and range of the test scores: 80, 85, 90, 85, 70.
steps: Median: the middle of five numbers is the 3rd one → 85.

modeledExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median, mode, and range of reading minutes: 10, 12, 15, 18.
steps: Median: with four numbers, average the two middles → (12 + 15) ÷ 2 = 13.5.
Mode: every number appears once → there is no mode.

practice.work: 4 appears three times, more than any other value.

practice.prompt: Test scores: 88, 92, 100, 88, 76, 96. Find the mean, median, mode, and range.
Daily steps: 5000, 7000, 6000, 8000, 6000. Find the mean, median, mode, and range.
For 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 100, would the mean or the median better describe a typical value? Explain.
practice.answer: The median (15) better describes a typical value because 100 is an outlier that pulls the mean up to about 28.3.
practice.rubric: Full credit: names the median AND explains that 100 is an outlier raising the mean.

practice.prompt: Create your own data set of 5 numbers that has a mode of 9 and a range of 10.
practice.rubric: Full credit: 5 numbers where 9 appears most often AND (greatest − least) = 10.

answerKey.answer: Median (≈15) describes a typical value better than the mean (≈28.3) because 100 is an outlier.
Sample: 5, 9, 9, 12, 15 (mode 9, range 10). Other correct sets accepted.

exitTicket.prompt: In one sentence, explain the difference between range and mode.
exitTicket.answer: Range is greatest minus least (how spread out); mode is the value that appears most often. Accept any correct comparison.

lesson.reflection: When would the median describe a data set better than the mean? Use the word outlier in your answer.

misconceptions.misconception: Finding the median without ordering the numbers first.
misconceptions.fix: Always rewrite the data least-to-greatest before picking the middle.

misconceptions.misconception: Thinking every data set has exactly one mode.
misconceptions.fix: A set can have no mode (all different) or more than one mode.

misconceptions.misconception: Confusing range (a spread) with the largest value.
misconceptions.fix: Range is greatest minus least, not just the biggest number.

esolSupports: Bilingual EN/ES vocabulary box with worked examples
Color-code or underline the middle number when finding the median
spedSupports: Provide the data already ordered for the first few problems
Reduce to problems 1–4 plus one multi-part item
Allow a calculator for the mean
lesson.extension: Give students a data set and ask them to add one value that does NOT change the median but DOES change the mean.
lesson.subNotes: Fully self-running: read the two modeled examples aloud, then have students complete the practice page. Answer key included for grading.
